courseTitle: Complex Numbers & Quadratic Equations — Class XI Crash Course

modules.title: Complex Numbers
modules.hook: $x^2+1=0$ has no real solution — every real number squared is $\ge 0$, so it can never equal $-1$. Complex numbers are the number system built specifically to fix that gap.
modules.definition: A number of the form $a+ib$, where $a$ and $b$ are real numbers, is called a complex number. Here $a$ is the real part (written $\text{Re}\,z$) and $b$ is the imaginary part (written $\text{Im}\,z$).

concepts.heading: Why we need i at all
concepts.body: The equation $x^2+1=0$ has no real solution — every real number squared is $\ge0$, so it can never equal $-1$. Defining $i=\sqrt{-1}$ (so $i^2=-1$) extends the number system just enough to solve equations like this.

concepts.heading: Equality of complex numbers
concepts.body: Two complex numbers $z_1=a+ib$ and $z_2=c+id$ are equal ONLY when BOTH parts match: $a=c$ AND $b=d$. One equation in complex numbers secretly hides two ordinary real equations.

video.caption: Coming soon: why √-1 needed its own symbol

tricks: Every real number is also a complex number (with $b=0$) — reals are a special case, not a separate universe.
Equality of $a+ib=c+id$ splits into two real equations: $a=c$ and $b=d$ — always solve them separately.

quiz.q: Two complex numbers $a+ib=c+id$ are equal when:
Is every real number also a complex number?
Which equation has NO real solution but IS solvable using $i$?

modules.title: Algebra of Complex Numbers
modules.hook: Adding, subtracting, and multiplying complex numbers works almost exactly like ordinary algebra — with one rule that changes everything mid-calculation: whenever $i^2$ shows up, it becomes $-1$.
modules.definition: Complex numbers add, subtract, multiply and divide almost exactly like ordinary algebraic expressions — with one rule that changes everything: whenever $i^2$ appears, replace it with $-1$.

concepts.heading: Addition, subtraction, multiplication
concepts.body: $z_1+z_2=(a+c)+i(b+d)$ — add the parts separately. $z_1-z_2=(a-c)+i(b-d)$. $z_1z_2=(ac-bd)+i(ad+bc)$ — expand like any binomial product, then replace $i^2$ with $-1$.

concepts.heading: Powers of i cycle every 4
concepts.body: $i^1=i, i^2=-1, i^3=-i, i^4=1$, then it repeats: $i^5=i, i^6=-1,\dots$. For any integer $k$: $i^{4k}=1$, $i^{4k+1}=i$, $i^{4k+2}=-1$, $i^{4k+3}=-i$. To simplify $i^n$ for large $n$, just find the remainder when $n$ is divided by 4.

concepts.heading: Square roots of negative reals
concepts.body: For a positive real $a$: $\sqrt{-a}=\sqrt{a}\,i$. **Warning**: the rule $\sqrt{a}\times\sqrt{b}=\sqrt{ab}$ does NOT hold when both $a,b$ are negative — treating $\sqrt{-1}\times\sqrt{-1}$ as $\sqrt{1}=1$ gives a contradiction; it's actually $i\times i=i^2=-1$.

video.caption: Coming soon: the powers-of-i cycle, visualized

tricks: To simplify $i^n$ for a big exponent n: divide n by 4, the REMAINDER tells you the answer ($i^0=1,i^1=i,i^2=-1,i^3=-i$).
Division of complex numbers: multiply top and bottom by the conjugate of the denominator to clear the $i$ from underneath.
$\sqrt{-a}=\sqrt{a}\,i$ for positive $a$ — never write $\sqrt{-a}=-\sqrt{a}$, that's a completely different (wrong) thing.

options: The rule √a·√b=√ab only holds when a,b aren't both negative
Square roots don't exist for negative numbers

modules.title: Modulus and Conjugate
modules.hook: Modulus is just distance from home — the same Pythagoras formula you've used for years. Conjugate is your mirror image: same real part, imaginary part flipped.
modules.definition: For $z=a+ib$, the modulus $|z|$ is the non-negative real number $\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$, and the conjugate $\bar z$ is $a-ib$ — same real part, imaginary part flipped in sign.

concepts.heading: Modulus is just distance from the origin
concepts.body: $|z|=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ is exactly the Pythagorean distance formula — it measures how far the point $(a,b)$ is from $(0,0)$.

concepts.heading: The conjugate trick for division
concepts.body: $z\bar z=|z|^2$ is always a real number (no leftover $i$). This is exactly why dividing by a complex number works: multiply top and bottom by the conjugate of the denominator, and the denominator becomes a plain real number.

video.caption: Coming soon: why the conjugate trick clears the i

tricks: $z\bar z=|z|^2$ — multiplying by the conjugate always eliminates $i$ from the denominator.
$|z_1z_2|=|z_1||z_2|$ and $\left|\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right|=\frac{|z_1|}{|z_2|}$ — modulus behaves like ordinary absolute value under multiplication and division.
$\overline{z_1z_2}=\bar z_1\bar z_2$ and $\overline{z_1\pm z_2}=\bar z_1\pm\bar z_2$ — conjugate distributes over both operations.

examples.question: Find the conjugate of $\frac{(3-2i)(2+3i)}{(1+2i)(2-i)}$.
steps: So the expression is $\frac{12+5i}{4+3i}$; multiply by $\frac{4-3i}{4-3i}$: $\frac{(12+5i)(4-3i)}{16+9}=\frac{48-36i+20i+15}{25}=\frac{63-16i}{25}$

quiz.q: Why multiply by the conjugate when dividing complex numbers?
options: It turns the denominator into a real number ($z\bar z=|z|^2$)
It's just a shortcut with no real reason

modules.title: Argand Plane
modules.hook: A complex number is basically a map pin — the real part is your east-west position, the imaginary part is north-south. Plot it exactly like any other coordinate.
modules.definition: Every complex number $x+iy$ corresponds to a unique point $P(x,y)$ in a plane — called the Argand plane (or complex plane). The x-axis is the real axis, the y-axis is the imaginary axis.
notation: Real axis = x-axis, Imaginary axis = y-axis
$\bar z \leftrightarrow$ mirror image of P across the real axis

concepts.heading: Plotting complex numbers as points
concepts.body: $2+4i$, $-2+3i$, $0+1i$, $2+0i$, $-5-2i$ and $1-2i$ correspond to the ordered pairs $(2,4)$, $(-2,3)$, $(0,1)$, $(2,0)$, $(-5,-2)$, $(1,-2)$ — plot them exactly like coordinates.

diagram.caption: Six complex numbers plotted on the Argand plane

concepts.heading: Modulus is literally distance on the plane
concepts.body: $|z|=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$ is exactly the straight-line distance from the origin $O(0,0)$ to the point $P(x,y)$ — the Pythagorean distance formula, nothing new.

concepts.heading: Conjugate is the mirror image
concepts.body: The conjugate $\bar z=x-iy$ corresponds to the point $Q(x,-y)$ — the reflection of $P(x,y)$ across the real axis, like looking in a mirror laid along the x-axis.

diagram.caption: z and its conjugate — mirrored across the real axis

video.caption: Coming soon: plotting complex numbers live

tricks: Points on the x-axis are complex numbers of the form $a+i0$ (purely real); points on the y-axis are $0+ib$ (purely imaginary).
Conjugation never moves a point left/right — only flips it above/below the real axis.
Modulus is ALWAYS non-negative — it's a distance, and distances can't be negative.

examples.question: Plot $z=1-2i$ on the Argand plane and find $|z|$.

quiz.q: Which axis represents the imaginary part of a complex number?
The conjugate of a point P(x,y) on the Argand plane is:
$|z|$ for z corresponding to point (3,4) is:
A point on the y-axis represents a complex number of the form:
Can the modulus of a complex number ever be negative?
options: Yes, if the imaginary part is negative
Yes, if both parts are negative

questions.q: A point on the Argand plane's x-axis represents:
